title: Ciabatta, high hydration
subtitle: The open-crumbed Italian loaf that needs a wet dough and patience
excerpt: A ciabatta at 82% hydration: strong flour, a poolish pre-ferment, and enough olive oil to carry the flavour. The dough is too wet to knead in the traditional sense; stretch-and-fold sets the structure instead. Two loaves from one batch, each with the open, irregular crumb the bread is known for.
sourceNotes: Ciabatta is a northern Italian bread, documented from the 1980s in the Veneto and Liguria. Its defining characteristic is the high hydration (80 to 85%) that produces the open, irregular crumb and flat, slipper shape. No British public-domain source covers it; the recipe here draws on the standard Italian regional technique: poolish pre-ferment (50% of the flour), 82% total hydration, olive oil in the final dough, stretch-and-fold development, retard in the fridge for flavour, shaped cold and baked in a very hot oven. The baker's percentages : hydration 82%, salt 2%, yeast 0.4% total (split between poolish and final dough). Follow the Ligurian tradition for a lean, flavourful ciabatta.

content.text: Ciabatta at 82% hydration is an exercise in working with a dough that doesn't behave like any other bread dough. It won't hold a shape; it pours more than it tears; it sticks to everything unless you keep your hands and surfaces well-floured or wet. The payoff is a crumb that is truly open, large, irregular holes, a thin crust that shatters when you cut it, a flavour deepened by a poolish pre-ferment and a long cold retard.

The method runs across two days. On day one, build the poolish and leave it overnight. On day two, mix the final dough, do four rounds of stretch-and-fold, and chill the dough overnight. On day three, shape the cold loaves (the chill makes the wet dough handleable), proof them at room temperature for about an hour, and bake in a very hot oven with steam. Domestic ovens drift, an oven thermometer at 240°C is worth checking before these go in.

attrs.body: Ciabatta dough at 82% hydration will stick to anything that isn't well-floured or well-oiled. When you tip the dough out to divide it, flour the bench heavily first, flour the top of the dough before you handle it, and keep the bench scraper wet. Work quickly; the dough relaxes on contact with a surface and starts spreading.
attrs.title: Flour your surface generously

content.text: What you need
Method
Day one: build the poolish
Stir 1 g of fast-action yeast into 250 ml room-temperature water until dissolved. Add the 250 g of flour and stir until no dry flour remains. The
poolish

should be a thick batter, not a dough. Cover with cling film and leave at room temperature for 12 to 16 hours. When it is ready, the surface will be domed and covered with small bubbles, and it should smell pleasantly sour.
Day two: mix the final dough

Dissolve the remaining 1 g of yeast in the 160 ml of water. Combine the poolish and the yeasted water in a large bowl. Add the remaining 250 g of strong bread flour and stir until no dry flour remains. Add the 10 g of salt and the 20 ml of olive oil. The dough is very wet and sticky; this is correct. Cover and rest for 30 minutes.
Stretch and fold, four rounds

Over the next 2 hours, do four rounds of stretch and fold with 30 minutes between each round. For each round: wet your hand, reach under one side of the dough, lift it up, and fold it over the top. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat until you have come all the way around, that is one round. After the third or fourth round the dough should feel noticeably more elastic and hold its shape briefly before relaxing.
Cold retard, overnight
After the fourth fold, cover the bowl tightly and refrigerate for 8 to 12 hours. The cold slows fermentation and makes the dough handleable enough to divide and shape on day three.
Day three: divide and shape

Preheat the oven to 240°C conventional (or as high as your oven goes). Place a roasting tin on the oven floor to heat through. Flour the work surface very generously. Tip the cold dough out and flour the top. Using a bench scraper, divide the dough into two equal pieces. Handle each piece as little as possible; the aim is to preserve the gas bubbles that have developed overnight. Fold each piece gently in thirds (like folding a letter) then place seam-side down on a well-floured baking tray lined with baking paper. Leave a
bench rest
of 15 minutes, then gently pull each piece to extend it to about 28 to 30 cm long. Don't press hard; let gravity help.
Proof, 45 to 60 minutes

Dust the shaped loaves with flour and leave uncovered at room temperature for 45 to 60 minutes, until they look puffy and a gentle poke leaves a slow impression. Do not over-proof, ciabatta deflates easily at this stage.
Bake, 24 to 28 minutes

Drop a handful of ice cubes into the hot roasting tin on the oven floor to create steam. Slide the loaves into the oven. After 8 minutes, open the oven door briefly to release the steam, then continue baking for a further 16 to 20 minutes, until the loaves are deep golden-brown all over, sound hollow when tapped on the base, and feel very light for their size. The internal temperature should read 98°C. Transfer to a rack and leave for at least 30 minutes before cutting.
Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: The three problems that affect high-hydration breads most often.

items.fix: Four full rounds of stretch-and-fold are needed, not two or three. The dough should feel noticeably more elastic after each round. And it must be cold from the fridge when you shape it; room-temperature ciabatta dough at 82% hydration won't be handleable.
items.cause: Insufficient gluten development during stretch-and-fold, or the dough was not cold enough when shaped.
items.symptom: Dough collapses when shaped and won't hold any shape at all

items.fix: Handle the shaped loaves as little as possible. A poke should leave a slow indent, if the dough doesn't spring back at all, it has gone too far. Bake immediately rather than waiting. A very hot oven also helps oven spring.
items.cause: Over-proofed before baking, or deflated during shaping.
items.symptom: Dense crumb with no open holes

items.fix: 240°C is the minimum. Check with an oven thermometer. The roasting tin of ice cubes creates the burst of steam the crust needs to set before it dries; without it the crust is thick and pale.
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or insufficient steam in the first 8 minutes.

attrs.heading: When things go wrong
